caveats: a: No source measures current not-yet-automated AI task exposure for this six-digit industry or narrowed lens.
a: The provided compensation-to-receipts ratio of 0.1511 covers the broader code and combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts, so it may not represent smaller OEM plants.
rho: The sources establish use cases and constraints, not a five-year implementation rate.
rho: Implementation readiness differs sharply between modern high-volume lines and small legacy or specialty-load shops.
e: No denominator study identifies the outsourced-manufacturing share of LMM firms in NAICS 332992.
e: Type 07 firearms manufacturers may also make ammunition without a separate Type 06 license, complicating a license-based census.
e: The provided firm count of 23 is an estimated full-code count and may not map cleanly to diversified or multi-establishment parents.
s5: The observed deals are not lower-middle-market hazard-rate observations.
s5: Published transactions are biased toward large, strategic, and contested assets.
q: No source directly measures five-year retention of implemented AI benefit in the narrowed lens.
q: Commercial white-label, law-enforcement, and defense contracts have materially different pricing and qualification structures.
d5: The sources represent a large public producer and a government-owned production program, not the outsourced LMM population.
d5: Revenue changes combine price, mix, project revenue, and volume and therefore do not directly equal constant-quality demand.
o: This is bounded judgment, not an observed displacement rate.
o: Operator requirement does not imply that the current supplier, facility, or staffing level persists.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers place recurring production orders for loaded ammunition, cases, bullets, and related components. Revenue is generally per round, component, lot, or contract delivery, with specifications, testing, packaging, traceability, and sometimes program support incorporated into the price.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code mixes branded product businesses, captive or government-oriented production, and outsourced manufacturing. The lens is narrowed to OEM, contract, and white-label operators because their repeat external-customer manufacturing revenue forms a coherent outsourced-service model; the narrowing is not based on attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Proprietary ammunition brands without meaningful outsourced production revenue; captive government plants; firearms-only manufacturers; distributors and retailers; hobby reloaders; destructive-device or large-caliber ammunition; and firms outside the lower-middle-market control-acquisition band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market OEM, contract, and white-label manufacturers of small-arms ammunition and ammunition components that repeatedly produce to external brand, distributor, commercial, law-enforcement, or defense-customer specifications.

a.bridge: NIST describes AI use cases for small and medium manufacturers generally, not a wage-weighted task measure for small-arms ammunition. I map its predictive maintenance, predictive quality, scrap, throughput, and forecasting applications to the automated forming, loading, optical inspection, testing, packaging, scheduling, quality-record, quoting, and administrative workflows documented by ammunition manufacturers. This likely overstates substitution in hazardous physical production and understates avoidable hiring in planning and documentation.
caveats: No source measures current not-yet-automated AI task exposure for this six-digit industry or narrowed lens.
The provided compensation-to-receipts ratio of 0.1511 covers the broader code and combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts, so it may not represent smaller OEM plants.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted occupational roster and task-level time study from several eligible plants, separated into already automated, AI-addressable, and irreducibly physical work, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: AI exposure is concentrated in demand and material forecasting, schedule optimization, machine-health analysis, vision-inspection review, quality trend detection, document retrieval, quoting, and customer administration. Press operation, loading, changeover, material movement, ballistic testing, safety supervision, and physical inspection constrain the wage-weighted opportunity.

d5.bridge: Olin reports mixed current ammunition demand, with stronger military sales and weaker commercial and law-enforcement sales, while the Army's new 6.8 mm facility indicates modernization demand. Neither measures constant-price year-five demand for outsourced LMM production. I translate those segment and program signals to a roughly stable-to-growing physical-volume outlook. Large-prime military evidence may bias the estimate high for small OEMs, while recent commercial weakness may bias it low if channel inventories normalize.
caveats: The sources represent a large public producer and a government-owned production program, not the outsourced LMM population.
Revenue changes combine price, mix, project revenue, and volume and therefore do not directly equal constant-quality demand.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price shipment and physical-round data by commercial, law-enforcement, and military channel, plus OEM backlog, utilization, and funded procurement forecasts for eligible firms, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Training, defense readiness, law enforcement, hunting, and recreational shooting require consumable ammunition, and military modernization supports demand. Commercial cycles, inventories, regulation, export controls, raw-material availability, substitution among calibers, and government concentration create a wide range and credible downside.

caveats: No denominator study identifies the outsourced-manufacturing share of LMM firms in NAICS 332992.
Type 07 firearms manufacturers may also make ammunition without a separate Type 06 license, complicating a license-based census.
The provided firm count of 23 is an estimated full-code count and may not map cleanly to diversified or multi-establishment parents.
e.change_evidence: A complete ATF-licensee and establishment-to-parent census, matched to NAICS, revenue band, websites, customer disclosures, and OEM revenue share, would materially change this value.
e.rationale: OEM and white-label operators demonstrably exist, but much of the code consists of proprietary brands, vertically integrated producers, or government-oriented capacity. I estimate that about one-quarter of LMM-band firms derive sufficiently material recurring outsourced manufacturing revenue to meet the lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 332992 ($874,599,939) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 332992 ($6,169,946,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $421,301 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332992); class <5: 67 firms, avg revenue/firm $462,358 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $90,715 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 26 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,979,731 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $388,423 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,223,586 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $632,468 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,980,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,742,936 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 10 firms, avg revenue/firm $54,401,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,673,574 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 10 firms, avg revenue/firm $512,844,900 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $100,620,169 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 23

caveats: This is bounded judgment, not an observed displacement rate.
Operator requirement does not imply that the current supplier, facility, or staffing level persists.
o.change_evidence: A survey of brand, distributor, and defense customers on five-year insourcing, supplier consolidation, qualification, and sourcing commitments, together with announced captive-capacity additions, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: The service basket produces physical ammunition and components through forming, loading, inspection, testing, packaging, and delivery. Software can coordinate and improve these operations but cannot supply the physical product or remove licensed and accountable manufacturing. Limited insourcing or vertical integration prevents using exactly one as the base.

q.bridge: Olin's Winchester segment is a large branded and military supplier rather than an LMM contract manufacturer. Its reported effects from volumes, pricing, product mix, and raw-material or operating costs show commercial price sensitivity and margin volatility; I translate those dynamics to per-unit OEM contracts, allowing greater retention where qualification, precision, confidentiality, or scarce capacity creates switching friction. This may understate retention for specialty defense programs and overstate it for readily rebid commodity loads.
caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of implemented AI benefit in the narrowed lens.
Commercial white-label, law-enforcement, and defense contracts have materially different pricing and qualification structures.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm customer contracts and quote histories showing cost-escalation clauses, open-book terms, rebid cadence, qualification costs, gross-margin bridges, and churn after productivity changes would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Customers can benchmark common calibers and rebid per-round production, so visible labor savings should be shared through renewal pricing and competition. Operators can retain more benefit from reduced scrap, improved yield, faster qualification, fewer stoppages, and reliable delivery when these gains are not itemized and switching requires testing or scarce production capacity.

rho.bridge: NIST's implementation lessons cover manufacturers broadly, while ATF licensing and manufacturer process pages document sector constraints but not adoption rates. I translate incremental pilot feasibility, data requirements, automated inspection, and licensing or quality accountability into the implemented share of the exposed opportunity. The proxy may be high for firms with fragmented machine data and low for modern plants already collecting high-frequency inspection data.
caveats: The sources establish use cases and constraints, not a five-year implementation rate.
Implementation readiness differs sharply between modern high-volume lines and small legacy or specialty-load shops.
rho.change_evidence: Interviews with operations, quality, safety, and IT leaders at at least ten eligible firms, including data availability, pilot budgets, validation gates, and production deployment outcomes, would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Planning, maintenance triage, document search, and human-in-the-loop anomaly detection can be deployed incrementally. Safety-critical loading, ballistic acceptance, customer specifications, controlled technical data, traceability, and licensed-manufacturer accountability slow or prevent autonomous implementation.

s5.bridge: The AMMO manufacturing-asset acquisition and Kinetic Group control acquisition demonstrate transferable ammunition operations and buyer interest, but both are much larger than the target population and do not measure a five-year LMM hazard. I use them only as market evidence and apply a lower, wide ownership-cycle estimate. This may overstate LMM liquidity because strategic assets receive disproportionate attention and understate undisclosed small transactions.
caveats: The observed deals are not lower-middle-market hazard-rate observations.
Published transactions are biased toward large, strategic, and contested assets.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census from PitchBook, CapIQ, GF Data, Axial, bankruptcy sales, and specialist defense advisers, matched to a verified eligible-firm denominator and distinguishing control transfers from asset closures, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Recent acquisitions show that ammunition plants, brands, licenses, and capabilities can transfer. Limited target count, specialized facilities, environmental and product-liability diligence, customer or government dependence, security review, and owner concentration constrain the probability of a qualifying transfer in any fixed five-year period.

principal_driver: Consumable physical demand and machine-intensive, data-rich quality and production workflows create recurring need and targeted AI use cases.
principal_weakness: The eligible LMM OEM population is very small and weakly measured, while safety, regulation, and commoditized pricing restrict implementation and retention.
